raw_case:
Ureaplasma urealyticum pyelonephritis associated with renal, perinephric, retroperitoneal, and bladder abscesses in a hypogammaglobinemic adult patient presenting with gross hematuria and marked thrombocytosis
Ureaplasma urealyticum is an opportunistic bacterium that is often identified in asymptomatic, healthy individuals from colonization of the genitourinary tract. However, in immunocompromised patients with hypogammaglobulinemia, Ureaplasma has been reported to cause a wide range of infections, most commonly urethritis and cystitis. The absence of a bacterial cell wall prevents Ureaplasma from being cultured using routine bacteriologic media and makes it resistant to commonly prescribed antibiotics. As a result, delayed or missed diagnosis can occur with patients initially receiving inadequate medical management. The use of broad-range bacterial polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplification to detect bacterial ribonucleic acid is often required to make the diagnosis in suspected cases. We present a rare case of Ureaplasma urealyticum causing histopathologically-confirmed unilateral pyelonephritis with associated renal, perinephric, retroperitoneal, and bladder abscesses in a hypogammaglobinemic adult patient. We performed a literature review for cases of Ureaplasma urealyticum causing upper urinary tract infections and nephric-perinephric abscesses and found that these are extremely rare manifestations.

Introduction

Ureaplasma urealyticum, a bacterium belonging to the family Mycoplasmataceae, is commonly found in the genitourinary tracts of sexually active, healthy individuals, of which the majority are asymptomatic with genitourinary colonization [1]. However, it has previously been proposed that Ureaplasma may have enhanced virulence in immunocompromised individuals with hypogammaglobulinemia, with a limited number of case reports identifying Ureaplasma as the causative agent for urethritis, cystitis, polyarthritis, osteomyelitis, and pneumonia in patients with hypogammaglobulinemia [2], [3], [4], [5]. Primary hypogammaglobulinemia from a genetic condition like common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) has been associated with disseminated Ureaplasma infection [6], [7], [8]. With the recent expanded use of anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies, there has been an increased incidence of secondary hypogammaglobulinemia [9]. The overwhelming majority of reported Ureaplasma-related urinary tract infections in the literature involve the lower urinary tract (urethritis or cystitis) [10], [11], [12], [13], [14], [15], [16], [17], [18]. Currently, only a few case reports have identified Ureaplasma as the causative agent in immunocompromised adult patients with upper urinary tract infections (ureteritis or pyelonephritis) [19], [20], [21], [22]. We present a rare case of Ureaplasma urealyticum causing histopathologically-confirmed unilateral pyelonephritis with associated renal, perinephric, retroperitoneal, and bladder abscesses in an immunocompromised adult patient with hypogammaglobulinemia.

Case presentation

A 29-year-old Caucasian female with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis for which receiving anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody therapy (initially rituximab and more recently ocrelizumab) over the past four years every 6 months, presented to the hospital with chronic gross hematuria and multiple non-specific symptoms including fatigue, weakness, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, and lightheadedness. Other past medical history included diabetes mellitus type 1 which was well-controlled on an insulin pump, hypothyroidism, and severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection without respiratory symptoms diagnosed ten days prior to presentation. She reported a history of chronic gross hematuria of unclear etiology that began nine months prior to presentation for which she was previously seen by an outside urologist and was diagnosed with interstitial cystitis. Physical exam at time of presentation revealed blood pressure 95/61 mmHg, heart rate 116 beats per minute, temperature 37.1 °C, and mild crackles of the posterior lung bases bilaterally. Laboratory workup (Table 1) demonstrated a white blood cell (WBC) count of 18,700 WBCs/mcL with 83 % neutrophils, platelet count of 842,000 platelets/mcL, hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL, serum creatinine of 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR of 87 mL/min/BSA per CKD-EPI), serum Cystatin C of 1.10 mg/L (eGFR of 72 mL/min/BSA per CKD-EPI), serum albumin of 3.3 g/dL, and hemoglobin A1c of 6.2 %. Urinalysis with microscopy obtained on admission revealed > 182 WBC/hpf, 151 RBC/hpf, WBC clumps present, large leukocyte esterase, negative nitrite, negative glucose. Urine culture and blood cultures (bacterial and fungal) obtained prior to administration of any antimicrobial agents were no growth. Urine pregnancy test was negative. Chest x-ray showed ground-glass changes of the mid and lower interstitial lung spaces. Given her gross hematuria, a CT urogram was obtained which revealed multifocal left upper pole and interpolar renal pyelonephritis with an associated large 9.5 cm left nephric-perinephric abscess extending into the left retroperitoneum, an anterior lateral 2.5 cm intraparenchymal left renal abscess, cystitis and left ureteritis, and moderate bilateral hydroureteronephrosis (left greater than right). Subsequent MRI of the pelvis also showed findings of an anterior-superior bladder abscess. As she met criteria for sepsis with pyelonephritis and associated renal, perinephric, retroperitoneal, and bladder abscesses, she was started on broad-spectrum antimicrobial coverage, initially with piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy but later broadened to include combination of a carbapenem, vancomycin, and amphotericin B after she developed high-grade sustained fever and persistent leukocytosis. She underwent cystoscopy by urology with left ureteral stent placement given findings of hydronephrosis on CT urogram. She underwent aspiration with drain placement by interventional radiology of her left perinephric-retroperitoneal abscess, left superior-anterior renal abscess, and anterior-superior bladder abscess, all of which had purulent output. Despite having purulent fluid from all these drainage sites, gram stains were negative and cultures (bacterial, mycobacterial, and fungal) all remained no growth with exception of Candida glabrata from initial drainage of the anterior-superior bladder abscess. Testing for Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, human immunodeficiency virus, Coccidioides, Cryptococcus, Blastomyces, and Histoplasma were all negative. Cytology of all sites was unremarkable. Given her severe and persistent thrombocytosis (reaching up to 1028,000 platelets/mcL) with associated leukocytosis (reaching up to 26,900 WBCs/mcL) and anemia (requiring transfusion of 2 units packed red blood cells) over the course of her hospitalization, hematology was consulted. After extensive workup to evaluate for possible reactive and autonomous causes (Table 1), the patient’s severe thrombocytosis and leukocytosis was contributed to reactive process from her underlying infection. Testing for serum immunoglobulins (Ig) was performed given her use of ocrelizumab which revealed hypogammaglobulinemia with low IgG of 445 mg/dL (normal 767–1590), low IgM of 23 mg/dL (normal 37–286), and normal IgA of 133 mg/dL (normal 61–356). Outside records revealed that that patient had normal serum immunoglobulins (IgG and IgM) before being started on ocrelizumab. During her hospitalization, she received intravenous immunoglobulin 25 g x2 doses separated 3 days apart with subsequent normalization of her serum IgG to 1050 mg/dL. As she remained febrile with persistent leukocytosis and no clinical improvement despite aspiration of abscesses and broad-spectrum antibacterial and antifungal therapy, a left native renal biopsy was obtained. Histopathology revealed findings confirming pyelonephritis with marked suppurative inflammation (Fig. 1A & 1B). Grocott's methenamine silver (GMS) fungal stain (Fig. 1C) and acid-fast bacilli (AFB) stain (Fig. 1D) of the renal biopsy were both negative. Renal histopathology was not suggestive of xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis, inflammatory pseudotumor, malacoplakia, eosinophilic nephritis, or malignancy. Given persistent lack of any clinical improvement, patient ultimately underwent left-sided radical open nephrectomy. Broad-range bacterial PCR sequencing from the initial renal biopsy eventually returned positive for Ureaplasma urealyticum. Histopathology from the patient’s nephrectomy showed findings of pyelonephritis with organizing abscess and numerous cortical microabscesses (Fig. 2A & 2B). Repeat broad-range bacterial PCR sequencing from nephrectomy tissue was also positive for Ureaplasma urealyticum. Her antimicrobial regimen was subsequently adjusted to include doxycycline which she received 100 mg twice per day for a total of 7 days post-nephrectomy for targeted therapy of Ureaplasma. Following her left-sided radical nephrectomy and initiation of doxycycline therapy for coverage of Ureaplasma, she had resolution of her fevers, tachycardia, and leukocytosis, in addition to marked improvement of her thrombocytosis.

Discussion

Ureaplasma was first described in 1954 by Shepard after culturing this genus of bacteria from the urethras of men with non-gonococcal urethritis [23]. Ureaplasma species are unique in that they lack a bacterial cell wall. Consequently, Ureaplasma bacteria are osmotically fragile, cannot be visualized by routine gram staining, require a special culture medium containing sterols for growth, and are resistant to commonly prescribed antibiotics that target the bacterial cell wall [23], [24]. It is estimated that Ureaplasma is found on the mucosal surfaces of the genital tract (vagina and cervix) from colonization in 40–80 % of asymptomatic adult women [23], [25]. Immunocompromised individuals with hypogammaglobulinemia have an increased risk of this opportunistic pathogen causing genitourinary and extragenital infections [22]. As Ureaplasma species are resistant to beta lactams, sulfonamides, aminoglycosides, and clindamycin, antibiotic treatment requires the use of tetracyclines, macrolides, or fluoroquinolones.Currently, Ureaplasma is not medically recognized as a potential causative bacterium for pyelonephritis and nephric-perinephric abscesses. This lack of recognition is partly due to the previously mentioned challenges faced with making the diagnosis and the few reported human cases described in medical literature. Previous research by Pickering showed that a single reflux challenge of Ureaplasma urealyticum into rat kidneys resulted in histopathological changes consistent with Ureaplasma urealyticum-induced chronic pyelonephritis [26]. A thorough literature review revealed only 4 prior reported human cases of upper urinary tract infections from Ureaplasma, all of which involved immunocompromised female patients [19], [20], [21], [22]. Yazdani described an immunocompromised female with right pyelonephritis and perinephric abscess from Ureaplasma
[19]. Ngoo reported an immunocompromised female with multiple left renal and perinephric abscesses from Ureaplasma
[20]. Schwartz described an immunocompromised female with pyelonephritis from Ureaplasma
[21]. Jhaveri reported an immunocompromised female with bilateral nephric abscesses from Ureaplasma
[22]. Similar to our case, in all 4 of these case reports, there was no organism identified using standard microscopy, and cultures from all drained abscesses, blood, and urine samples using routine media were no growth. Also like our case, the diagnosis in three of these case reports (Yazdani, Ngoo, and Schwartz) required the use of broad-range bacterial PCR sequencing, whereas the fourth (Jhaveri) used “specialized urine culture techniques” to isolate the organism [19], [20], [21], [22].Our patient was immunocompromised based on her use of ocrelizumab, an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody, for her multiple sclerosis which likely contributed to her hypogammaglobinemia. Most cases of infection related Ureaplasma have been associated with hypogammaglobinemia from the use of anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies [22]. Chronic urethritis-cystitis has been previously associated with Ureaplasma infection in hypogammaglobulinemic patients [2]. Ureaplasma species have known IgA protease activity which has been proposed as a potential virulence factor given that IgA is the predominant immunoglobulin secreted by mucosal surfaces [23], [27], [28]. Additional research is needed to further understand the mechanism in which Ureaplasma as an opportunistic pathogen can result in genitourinary and extragenital infections in certain immunocompromised individuals. Future research should also explore why females appear to be more prone to developing localized and systemic infections over males.

Conclusion

We present a case of Ureaplasma urealyticum causing pyelonephritis with numerous associated genitourinary abscesses (nephric, perinephric, retroperitoneal, and bladder) as evidenced by renal histopathology and broad-range bacterial PCR sequencing. This case report highlights the importance of considering atypical organisms like Ureaplasma and Mycoplasma as causative infectious agents in hypogammaglobinemic patients with obvious signs of infection but negative bacterial and fungal cultures using routine media. This case report also highlights the utility of using broad-range bacterial PCR sequencing to detect and identify culture-negative bacterial infections. Medical providers should recognize Ureaplasma as a potential causative bacterium for pyelonephritis and genitourinary-associated abscesses in hypogammaglobinemic patients, especially given the recent expanded use of anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies.

Ethics

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and accompanying histopathology images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and accompanying histopathology images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Funding

No funding was needed or received for the creation of this manuscript.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

LWB, VCZ, AEM, and STA contributed equally to the conceptualization of the manuscript. LWB wrote the original draft of the manuscript. LWB, VCZ, AEM, and STA reviewed and edited the manuscript. STA provided the images in the manuscript.LWB, VCZ, AEM, and STA take responsibility that this study has been reported honestly, accurately and transparently, and accepts accountability for the overall work by ensuring that questions pertaining to the accuracy or integrity of any portion of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.

Declaration of Competing Interest

The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Sam T Albadri reports a relationship with TFS HealthScience that includes: consulting or advisory. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:**
  - 29-year-old Caucasian female
- **Chief Complaint:** 
  - Chronic gross hematuria and multiple non-specific symptoms (fatigue, weakness, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, lightheadedness)
- **History of Present Illness:**
  - 9 months of gross hematuria with a prior diagnosis of interstitial cystitis. Persistent symptoms despite prior care.
- **Past Medical History:**
  - Relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis treated with anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies (rituximab, ocrelizumab) for 4 years.
  - Type 1 diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, and recent SARS-CoV-2 infection 10 days prior.
- **Personal History:** 
  - None stated.
- **Family History:**
  - None stated.
- **Physical Examination:**
  - Blood pressure: 95/61 mmHg, heart rate: 116 bpm, temperature: 37.1°C, mild posterior lung base crackles.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Lab findings: Leukocytosis (18,700 WBCs/mcL), thrombocytosis (platelets 842,000/mcL), anemia (hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL), elevated serum Cystatin C (1.10 mg/L), sterile pyuria and hematuria on urinalysis, low IgG (445 mg/dL), low IgM (23 mg/dL).
  - Imaging: CT urogram showing left pyelonephritis with abscess formation (nephric, perinephric, retroperitoneal, bladder), hydroureteronephrosis, and findings of cystitis; chest X-ray with ground-glass changes.
  - Microbiology: Blood and urine cultures negative, Candida glabrata isolated from bladder abscess, broad-range bacterial PCR from renal biopsy and nephrectomy tissue positive for *Ureaplasma urealyticum*.
  - Biopsy: Renal histopathology confirmed pyelonephritis with microabscesses.
  - Other: Negative for common pathogens (e.g., *Chlamydia trachomatis*, *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*, HIV, fungal serologies).

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - Pyelonephritis complicated by abscesses, based on imaging and clinical findings of sepsis. Possible etiologies considered: bacterial (e.g., *Escherichia coli*, *Klebsiella*), fungal (e.g., *Candida*), mycobacterial, or atypical organisms.
   - Gross hematuria raised concerns for malignancy or severe infection.

2. **Diagnostic Tests and Observations:**
   - Blood and urine cultures showed no growth, ruling out most common uropathogens.
   - Persistent thrombocytosis and leukocytosis supported an infectious etiology with a reactive hematologic response.
   - Candida identified in bladder abscess, but fungal serologies and histopathology did not support widespread fungal infection.
   - Broad-range bacterial PCR identified *Ureaplasma urealyticum*, aligning with the patient's immunosuppressed status and unresponsiveness to standard antibiotics.

3. **Exclusion of Other Diagnoses:**
   - Negative results for acid-fast bacilli and fungal stains ruled out tuberculosis and fungal infections.
   - Imaging excluded malignancy and rarer inflammatory conditions (e.g., xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis).
   - Hypogammaglobulinemia from ocrelizumab use predisposed the patient to opportunistic infections, supporting the final diagnosis.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
- Disseminated *Ureaplasma urealyticum* infection causing unilateral pyelonephritis with nephric-perinephric, retroperitoneal, and bladder abscesses, in the context of hypogammaglobulinemia secondary to anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody therapy.

---
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Disseminated Ureaplasma urealyticum pyelonephritis with abscesses.

results.content:
### Resoning:

#### Step 1: Initial Assessment of Symptoms and History

The patient is a 29-year-old Caucasian female presenting with chronic gross hematuria lasting for 9 months and multiple non-specific symptoms including fatigue, weakness, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, and lightheadedness. She has a history of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis treated with anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies (rituximab and ocrelizumab) for 4 years, type 1 diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, and a recent SARS-CoV-2 infection 10 days prior.

Given the chronic nature of the hematuria and the presence of multiple systemic symptoms, it is crucial to consider a broad differential diagnosis that could explain these findings, taking into account her immunocompromised state due to the use of anti-CD20 therapies.

#### Step 2: Review of Past Medical History and Immunosuppressive Therapy

The patient has been on anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies for multiple sclerosis, which deplete B cells, potentially making her more susceptible to certain infections, particularly those that are opportunistic or atypical. The recent SARS-CoV-2 infection could also be a confounding factor, as it may have impacted her immune system further.

Given this immunocompromised state, infections with unusual pathogens or reactivation of latent infections should be considered in the differential diagnosis.

#### Step 3: Analysis of Physical Examination Findings

The physical examination reveals a blood pressure of 95/61 mmHg, heart rate of 116 bpm, temperature of 37.1°C, and mild posterior lung base crackles. These findings suggest possible systemic inflammation or infection, with the tachycardia and mild fever being particularly notable.

The blood pressure is on the lower side, which could be due to dehydration, blood loss, or sepsis. The tachycardia may be a compensatory response to these conditions.

#### Step 4: Interpretation of Laboratory Test Results

Lab findings include leukocytosis (18,700 WBCs/mcL), thrombocytosis (842,000/mcL), anemia (hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL), elevated serum cystatin C (1.10 mg/L), sterile pyuria and hematuria on urinalysis, and low immunoglobulins (IgG 445 mg/dL, IgM 23 mg/dL). These results suggest an ongoing inflammatory process or infection, possibly with some degree of renal impairment given the elevated cystatin C.

The low immunoglobulin levels are consistent with the effects of anti-CD20 therapy, which depletes B cells and can lead to hypogammaglobulinemia, increasing susceptibility to infections.

#### Step 5: Evaluation of Imaging Studies

CT urogram shows left pyelonephritis with abscess formation (nephric, perinephric, retroperitoneal, bladder), hydroureteronephrosis, and findings of cystitis. Chest X-ray shows ground-glass changes. These imaging findings are highly suggestive of a severe urinary tract infection with complications, including abscess formation and possible extension into adjacent structures.

The presence of ground-glass changes on chest X-ray could indicate pulmonary involvement, possibly from hematogenous spread of infection or concurrent pulmonary pathology.

#### Step 6: Review of Microbiology and Pathology Results

Blood and urine cultures are negative, but Candida glabrata is isolated from the bladder abscess. Broad-range bacterial PCR from renal biopsy and nephrectomy tissue is positive for Ureaplasma urealyticum. Renal histopathology confirms pyelonephritis with microabscesses.

The isolation of Candida glabrata from the bladder abscess and the detection of Ureaplasma urealyticum in renal tissue suggest that these organisms could be involved in the infection. However, the role of each in the patient's clinical presentation needs to be carefully evaluated.

#### Step 7: Consideration of Differential Diagnosis

Given the clinical presentation, laboratory findings, and imaging results, the differential diagnosis should consider infectious causes, particularly those that can cause severe urinary tract infections with abscess formation, especially in an immunocompromised host.

Potential pathogens include bacteria, fungi, and atypical organisms like Ureaplasma urealyticum. The presence of Candida glabrata and Ureaplasma urealyticum in the context of the patient's immunocompromised state makes these likely candidates.

#### Step 8: Evaluation of Treatment Options and Past Management

The patient was previously diagnosed with interstitial cystitis, but her symptoms persist despite prior care. Given the severity of her current condition, with abscess formation and systemic symptoms, a more aggressive approach to diagnosis and treatment is warranted.

The use of anti-CD20 therapy may have contributed to her susceptibility to infection and could impact the choice and duration of antimicrobial therapy.

#### Step 9: Integration of All Data to Reach a Diagnosis

Considering the chronic hematuria, systemic symptoms, immunocompromised state, imaging findings of pyelonephritis with abscesses, and microbiology results indicating Candida glabrata and Ureaplasma urealyticum, the most likely diagnosis is a complicated urinary tract infection due to Ureaplasma urealyticum with possible Candida glabrata superinfection, leading to pyelonephritis and abscess formation.

The role of Ureaplasma urealyticum in urinary tract infections is not well established, but given its detection in the renal tissue and the patient's clinical picture, it is plausible that this organism is contributing to her condition.

### Answer:

Complicated urinary tract infection due to Ureaplasma urealyticum with possible Candida glabrata superinfection, leading to pyelonephritis and abscess formation.
